answer:
**Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq's ghazal** from *Diwan-E-Zauq* (Chapter 4):<sup>[1](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/)</sup>

> [p-c45524] So frayed with dread, I say that I will die and be gone;<sup>[2](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-c45524)</sup>  
> If even death brings no relief, where then will I be gone?  
>   
> [p-ce8437] If you decide the rival's house is where you will be known,<sup>[3](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-ce8437)</sup>  
> Then my own firm resolves will change, and I will be gone.  
>   
> [p-6b6af7] O Healers, though your arts are spent and all your skills are shown,<sup>[4](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-6b6af7)</sup>  
> My wounds are not the kind that heal; they will not be gone.  
>   
> [p-b2e0da] How can we hope to reach her path, to make her lane our own?<sup>[5](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-b2e0da)</sup>  
> Not till we pass beyond both worlds, and finally are gone.  
>   
> [p-4965ab] My sigh could flash like lightning from a fiery, angry throne,<sup>[6](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-4965ab)</sup>  
> But I fear she would see the light and, startled, would be gone.  
>   
> [p-44c54e] I'm not the one to blame you for my final, dying groan;<sup>[7](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-44c54e)</sup>  
> In fact, if God himself should ask, I'll lie and be gone.  
>   
> [p-bb1675] The very fires of Hell itself will lose their burning tone,<sup>[8](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-bb1675)</sup>  
> When this poor sinner, drenched in shame, has finally been gone.  
>   
> [p-8fe6b9] No one will ever find a trace of me, no shard or bone,<sup>[9](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-8fe6b9)</sup>  
> When swift as a glance's flight from here, I will be gone.  
>   
> [p-223547] Tell all the rivers, when they see my pearl-shedding eyes alone,<sup>[10](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-223547)</sup>  
> If they should rise in flood, they'll fall from grace and will be gone.  
>   
> [p-f5a7e8] Should drunken friends bring their red eyes to my cold grave-stone,<sup>[11](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-f5a7e8)</sup>  
> At least two flowers will be laid there, and then they will be gone.  
>   
> [p-c7e243] Just lift the veil from your bright face, make its true radiance known;<sup>[12](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-c7e243)</sup>  
> The sun and moon will lose all worth, their glory will be gone.  
>   
> [p-80cec1] I too shall see if any have true vision of their own,<sup>[13](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-80cec1)</sup>  
> When swift as an arrow-glance from here, I finally am gone.  
>   
> [p-37950e] Zauq, those scholars whom the rigid schools have left to groan,<sup>[14](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/#p-37950e)</sup>  
> Bring them to the tavern's heart; they'll mend and be gone.

This ghazal's *matla* (opening couplet) expresses the lover's extreme distress—"frayed with dread," proclaiming death as an escape, yet doubting even that will bring peace (*mar jaayenge* / "I will die and be gone"). The refrain *jaayenge* ("will be gone") echoes through, weaving themes of inevitable departure, unhealable wounds of love, and ironic impermanence.<sup>[1](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/translation/)</sup>

**Transliteration** (for *matla*):  
Ab toh ghabra ke yeh kehte hain ke mar jaayenge  
Mar ke bhi chain na paaya toh kidhar jaayenge<sup>[15](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/transliteration/#p-7e0956)</sup>

**Original Urdu** (for *matla*):  
اب تو گھبرا کے یہ کہتے ہیں کہ مر جائیں_گے  
مر کے بھی چین نہ پایا تو کدھر جائیں_گے<sup>[16](https://falsafa.corpus/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/04-ab-toh-ghabra-ke-yeh-kehte-hain-ke-mar-jaayenge/original/#p-3b0a6c)</sup>
